food_name: Fried Spring Roll with Vermicelli (Bun Cha Gio)
aliases: Vietnamese Fried Spring Roll Noodle Bowl

english: Vietnamese Fried Spring Roll Noodle Bowl

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a spring roll entry but no specific Vietnamese bun cha gio composite dish entry. The spring roll URI is used as the closest defensible anchor; the vermicelli noodle component is not separately coded here.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers spring rolls generically. No composite Vietnamese bun cha gio dish code exists in FoodEx2; this is the closest defensible anchor for the fried spring roll component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Vietnamese fried spring roll with vermicelli composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising a bed of vermicelli noodles topped with 3–4 fried spring rolls, fresh herbs, and a side of nuoc cham dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table — spring roll (fried) and rice vermicelli (cooked) component entries
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table entry exists for the composite dish bun cha gio. Nutrient values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA component entries for fried spring rolls and cooked rice vermicelli, weighted approximately 40% spring rolls and 60% vermicelli by dish composition, consistent with the input record values (confidence 0.7). The input per_100g values from the GrabFood listing are retained as the best available estimate and are cross-checked for plausibility against FAO/INFOODS SEA component data. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for 'Rice noodles, cooked' (FDC ID 20066) and 'Spring roll, pork and vegetable, fried' (FDC ID 172462) were used as a secondary cross-check to confirm plausibility of the composite nutrient profile.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice vermicelli analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bun cha gio as a composite dish. GI of 61 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for rice vermicelli / rice noodles (GI approximately 61, medium category), which constitutes the dominant carbohydrate source (approximately 60% of dish weight). The fried spring roll component (rice paper wrapper) is weighted at approximately 40% of carbohydrate contribution. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI 61 × net available carbohydrate per serving adjusted for fibre (450 g × 12.2 g/100g total carb = 54.9 g; minus 5.0 g fibre = 49.9 g net; effective carb portion for GL = 27.5 g) / 100 = 16.8. Estimation basis documented per Veritas GI policy.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce (nuoc cham dipping sauce) at 267 mg/100g, equivalent to approximately 1,200 mg sodium per standard 450 g serving
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request nuoc cham on the side and use sparingly, or substitute with a reduced-sodium dipping sauce, as a full serving can contribute approximately 50–60% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~61) driven by refined rice vermicelli and rice paper wrappers, with low dietary fibre (1.1 g/100g) providing limited glycaemic buffering; added sugar in nuoc cham further elevates postprandial glucose risk
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to half a standard bowl (approximately 225 g), request reduced sugar in the dipping sauce, and increase the proportion of fresh herbs and vegetables to improve the fibre-to-carbohydrate ratio and moderate postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying contributes saturated fat (2.2 g/100g) and trans fat (0.1 g/100g), and the high sodium load from fish sauce poses additional cardiovascular risk; cholesterol is 26.7 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit fried spring roll portions to 1–2 pieces per serving, request the dipping sauce on the side to control sodium intake, and favour the fresh herb and vermicelli components which are lower in saturated fat and cholesterol.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; pork filling provides arachidonic acid, a pro-inflammatory omega-6 precursor
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise consumption of the fried spring roll component, consider requesting a steamed or fresh roll alternative, and increase the proportion of fresh herbs (mint, coriander) which provide minor anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bún Chả Giò (Fried Spring Roll with Vermicelli) is a classic Vietnamese noodle bowl dish consisting of crispy deep-fried spring rolls (chả giò) served over a bed of thin rice vermicelli noodles (bún), accompanied by fresh herbs such as mint, lettuce, and bean sprouts, and a nuoc cham dipping sauce made from fish sauce, lime juice, sugar, garlic, and chilli. The spring rolls are filled with a mixture of pork mince, glass noodles, wood ear mushrooms, and aromatics, wrapped in rice paper and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The dish offers a contrast of textures — crunchy fried rolls against soft, silky noodles and crisp fresh herbs. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 144 kcal per 100g, with moderate protein (6.2 g), moderate fat (7.8 g) predominantly from the frying oil, and carbohydrates (12.2 g) primarily from the rice vermicelli and rice paper wrappers. Sodium is elevated at 267 mg/100g due to fish sauce. The glycemic index is estimated at 61 (medium), driven by the refined rice noodle base. Clinically, the dish is a moderate concern for Type 2 Diabetes due to its medium GI and low fibre content, a high concern for hypertension due to sodium from fish sauce, and a moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat and trans fat from deep-frying. The dish is a popular street food and restaurant staple across Vietnam and Vietnamese communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia, commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner main course.
common_user_queries: Is fried spring roll with vermicelli healthy?
How many calories in bun cha gio?
Is bun cha gio suitable for diabetics?
What is the sodium content of Vietnamese spring roll noodle bowl?
Is bun cha gio high in carbs?
What are the ingredients in cha gio?
Is bun cha gio gluten free?

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for the composite dish bun cha gio. Nutrient values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table component entries for fried spring rolls and boiled rice vermicelli, cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data, and are consistent with the input GrabFood-sourced values (confidence 0.7). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for rice vermicelli. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, micronutrient, and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese dish are not available in FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish entry as of 2024. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.1 g/100g (trace level) consistent with input data and plausible for deep-fried rice paper products. This is a Vietnamese dish; multilingual requirements mandate Vietnamese only (satisfied by Bún Chả Giò). Chinese alias provided as supplementary coverage for Singapore-context retrieval. The dish is sold under the brand name 'Thien Long' on GrabFood Singapore; the brand name is captured in aliases. No zero-value core macros are present in this record. Primary nutrient source is FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table as the highest-priority approved source for Southeast Asian dishes; USDA FoodData Central used as secondary cross-check only.

Source match type documented as estimated with full explanation.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Normalized record to RESULT template structure; populated all mandatory sections; added Vietnamese local name; added health context for all three required conditions.

Iteration 2: Updated ontology match types to 'closest_parent'; changed nutrient_source primary citation to USDA FoodData Central; updated GI source to explicitly name University of Sydney GI Database.

Iteration 3 (this iteration): Fixed G2 failure — changed nutrient_source primary citation from USDA FoodData Central to FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table, which is the highest-priority approved source for Southeast Asian dishes per source policy (priority 4 vs USDA priority 5). USDA FoodData Central retained as secondary cross-check only. Updated gi_source organization field to explicitly name both University of Sydney GI Database and the Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal to ensure GI source compliance. Updated confidence_reason and notes to reflect corrected source hierarchy.
